agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "New 'Express Delivery' product offering time-sensitive deliveries with a claimed 2-hour window, premium pricing, and refund policy for missed windows. Synthesized from raw product discovery workshop notes involving PM, Operations, Finance, Customer Success, and Legal stakeholders.",
    "bounded_context": "Express Delivery Fulfillment — covers order eligibility, pickup dispatch, time-window enforcement, driver assignment, real-time tracking, and refund adjudication for express-tier orders.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Express Delivery", "definition": "A premium delivery tier with an expected 2-hour delivery window from order placement, available to customers within a defined radius of a warehouse." },
      { "term": "Express Fee", "definition": "A surcharge of $12.99 applied to express-tier orders, on top of the base delivery fee." },
      { "term": "Base Delivery Fee", "definition": "The standard delivery charge that applies to all orders regardless of tier. Not refundable under express SLA miss." },
      { "term": "Delivery Window", "definition": "The 2-hour period from order placement within which the express delivery is expected to be completed." },
      { "term": "Pickup SLA", "definition": "The 15-minute maximum time from order placement to physical pickup by a driver at the warehouse." },
      { "term": "Express-Eligible Driver", "definition": "A driver with a rating of 4.0 stars or above who is qualified to fulfill express orders." },
      { "term": "Warehouse Radius", "definition": "The 10km maximum distance from a warehouse within which a customer address must fall to qualify for express delivery." },
      { "term": "SLA Miss", "definition": "The event when an express delivery is not completed within the 2-hour delivery window, triggering refund evaluation." },
      { "term": "Wrong Address Exception", "definition": "A condition where the customer provided an incorrect delivery address, which voids the express refund eligibility." },
      { "term": "Driver Cancellation", "definition": "The event where an assigned express driver cancels the delivery mid-fulfillment, requiring immediate reassignment." },
      { "term": "Replacement Window", "definition": "The 10-minute maximum time to assign a replacement driver after a mid-delivery driver cancellation." },
      { "term": "Goodwill Refund", "definition": "DISPUTED TERM — Legal frames the express refund as a non-contractual goodwill gesture; PM and Customer Success frame it as a guarantee. Term intentionally flagged as unresolved." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A customer may only select Express Delivery if their delivery address is within 10km of an active warehouse.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Physical constraint — delivery within 2 hours is operationally impossible beyond this radius. Allowing ineligible orders corrupts the fulfillment pipeline.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a delivery address more than 10km from any active warehouse, the system MUST NOT allow Express Delivery selection."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "The express fee is $12.99, charged in addition to the base delivery fee.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Pricing is a business policy that can be changed by product/finance leadership.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an express order, the order total MUST include a line item of $12.99 labeled as the express fee, separate from the base delivery fee."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "An express order must be picked up by a driver within 15 minutes of order placement.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Operations states the 2-hour window cannot be met if pickup exceeds 15 minutes. This is a hard operational constraint that protects the entire SLA chain.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an express order placed at time T, a driver MUST arrive at the warehouse for pickup by T+15 minutes, or the order MUST be escalated."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Only drivers with a rating of 4.0 stars or above may be assigned to express orders.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Rating threshold is a quality policy. Operations could adjust the threshold (e.g., to 3.5 during driver shortages) without corrupting system state.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver with a rating below 4.0 stars, the system MUST NOT assign that driver to an express order."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "When an SLA miss occurs and the customer provided a valid address, only the express fee ($12.99) is refunded — never the base delivery fee.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Refunding the base fee would mean the delivery itself was free, which contradicts the cost model. The base fee covers actual logistics cost regardless of tier.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an SLA miss on a valid-address express order, the refund amount MUST equal exactly the express fee and MUST NOT include the base delivery fee."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "No express fee refund is issued if the customer provided an incorrect delivery address.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Customer fault voids the service commitment. Refunding despite customer error corrupts the accountability model.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an SLA miss where the delivery address was flagged as incorrect, the system MUST NOT issue an express fee refund."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "If a driver cancels mid-delivery on an express order, a replacement driver must be assigned within 10 minutes or the express fee is automatically refunded.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 10-minute replacement window and automatic refund trigger are operational policies that could be adjusted.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver cancellation on an active express delivery at time T, either a replacement driver is assigned by T+10 minutes OR the express fee refund is automatically initiated."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Express delivery customers must have access to real-time driver tracking from pickup through delivery.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Tracking is a product feature, not a data integrity constraint. It could be degraded gracefully without corrupting order state.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an active express delivery with a driver assigned, the customer MUST be able to view the driver's current location updated at least every 30 seconds."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Customer address is exactly 10.0km from the nearest warehouse.", "expected_behavior": "UNRESOLVED — need to define whether the 10km radius is inclusive (<=10km) or exclusive (<10km). Recommend inclusive." },
    { "scenario": "No express-eligible driver (4+ stars) is available at order placement.", "expected_behavior": "UNRESOLVED — should express option be hidden, or shown with a warning? If no eligible driver exists, the 15-minute pickup SLA will be breached. System should not offer express when it cannot fulfill." },
    { "scenario": "Customer places an express order and then changes their address after placement.", "expected_behavior": "UNRESOLVED — does a post-placement address change count as a 'wrong address' for refund voiding? Need policy decision." },
    { "scenario": "Driver picks up at minute 14, but delivery completes at 2 hours 1 minute.", "expected_behavior": "SLA miss triggers refund evaluation. The pickup SLA was met, so this is a transit-time failure. Refund should apply." },
    { "scenario": "Driver cancels at minute 115 of a 120-minute window — replacement cannot deliver in remaining 5 minutes.", "expected_behavior": "Replacement assigned within 10 min per BR-7, but SLA miss is guaranteed. Both BR-7 (auto-refund on failed reassignment) and BR-5 (SLA miss refund) apply. Only one refund should be issued — no double refund." },
    { "scenario": "Customer is within 10km of two warehouses.", "expected_behavior": "UNRESOLVED — which warehouse is authoritative for eligibility and dispatch? Need routing rules." },
    { "scenario": "Express order placed at 11:55 PM — does the 2-hour window span midnight or business day boundaries?", "expected_behavior": "UNRESOLVED — no mention of operating hours or blackout periods. Need to define express availability windows." },
    { "scenario": "How is 'wrong address' determined and by whom?", "expected_behavior": "UNRESOLVED — no process defined for adjudicating wrong-address claims. Driver assertion alone is insufficient; needs evidentiary standard to prevent abuse by either party." },
    { "scenario": "Refund processed within 3 business days but driver cancellation refund is 'automatic'.", "expected_behavior": "UNRESOLVED — 'automatic' may mean immediately initiated but still takes 3 business days to process, or it may mean instant credit. Need Finance to clarify." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Express fee ($12.99) creates a new revenue stream per order. However, SLA miss refunds are a direct cost. Without clear wrong-address adjudication (edge case), refund abuse could erode margins. The Legal vs PM contradiction on 'guarantee' has material financial risk — if marketing says 'guaranteed' and Legal says 'best effort', a class action for deceptive advertising is possible.",
    "operational": "15-minute pickup SLA and 4-star driver requirement create significant dispatch constraints. Express orders will compete with standard orders for the same driver pool, potentially degrading standard delivery quality. The 10-minute replacement window requires a real-time reassignment engine that likely does not exist today.",
    "user": "Real-time tracking and 2-hour window are strong value propositions. However, if the product is marketed as 'guaranteed' but Legal disclaims it, customer trust erodes on first SLA miss. The wrong-address exception could become a friction point in customer support if the adjudication process is opaque.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL: The guarantee vs best-effort contradiction is the highest-risk item. Building a product around a 'guarantee' that Legal will not defend exposes the company to regulatory and reputational risk. Secondary risk: no fallback defined when no eligible drivers are available — the system could accept orders it cannot fulfill.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Premium experience expectation set by 'guarantee' language; high dissatisfaction risk if SLA missed and refund denied (wrong address disputes)." },
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "4-star minimum creates a two-tier driver class. Express-eligible drivers carry higher pressure (15-min pickup). Ineligible drivers lose access to premium orders, creating rating anxiety." },
      { "group": "Warehouse Operations", "impact": "15-minute pickup SLA requires express orders to be prioritized in picking/packing, potentially disrupting standard order throughput." },
      { "group": "Finance", "impact": "Must build refund adjudication workflows. Refund volume directly impacts margin on express product. Need fraud detection for wrong-address gaming." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Directly contradicts PM on guarantee language. Must resolve before any customer-facing copy or marketing is produced." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will handle wrong-address disputes, SLA miss complaints, and tracking issues. Need clear escalation playbooks and adjudication authority." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 38,
    "reasoning": "Confidence is critically low due to a fundamental contradiction between PM ('guaranteed') and Legal ('best effort, not contractual') that affects the entire product's positioning, refund model, and customer communication. This is not a minor detail — it changes whether refunds are obligations or discretionary gestures, which cascades into every business rule about SLA enforcement. Additionally, the wrong-address adjudication process is completely undefined, and there is no fallback for driver unavailability. Cannot define acceptance criteria when the foundational promise of the product is contested between stakeholders.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Express fee pricing at $12.99",
      "10km warehouse radius eligibility constraint",
      "15-minute pickup SLA as an operational hard constraint",
      "4-star driver rating threshold for express eligibility",
      "Base delivery fee is never refunded on SLA miss"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether the 2-hour delivery is a guarantee or best-effort SLA — this changes the entire refund model",
      "Wrong-address determination process — who decides, what evidence, what appeals",
      "Automatic vs manual refund processing — Finance says 3 business days, Customer Success says automatic",
      "Express availability hours and blackout periods",
      "Behavior when no eligible driver is available"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The 10km radius is measured as straight-line distance from warehouse to delivery address, not driving distance",
      "Driver rating is a rolling average, not a lifetime rating — spec does not clarify",
      "The 2-hour window starts at order placement, not at pickup — inferred from Operations noting pickup must happen within first 15 minutes",
      "Only one warehouse is considered for dispatch per order — multi-warehouse routing is not in scope"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "PM states the 2-hour delivery is 'guaranteed' and the refund is a consequence of missing that guarantee. Legal explicitly states this is a 'best effort' SLA, not a contractual guarantee, and the refund is a 'goodwill gesture, not legally enforceable.' These are mutually exclusive framings that affect product positioning, marketing language, refund policy enforcement, and customer communication. This must be resolved before any implementation.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product Management", "Legal", "Marketing", "Customers", "Customer Support"],
      "options": [
        "A: Adopt Legal's framing — 'best effort' with discretionary goodwill refunds. Marketing cannot use 'guarantee' language.",
        "B: Adopt PM's framing — contractual guarantee with obligatory refunds. Legal must draft enforceable terms of service.",
        "C: Hybrid — market as 'Express Promise' (not 'guarantee'), with a published refund policy that is consistently applied but framed as policy, not contractual obligation."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C — 'Express Promise' avoids the legal liability of 'guarantee' while preserving the customer trust that PM wants. The refund policy is published and consistently applied (satisfying Customer Success) but framed as company policy (satisfying Legal). Requires Legal sign-off on specific language."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The process for determining 'wrong address' is completely undefined. Finance states refunds are voided for wrong addresses, but there is no definition of what constitutes a 'wrong' address, who adjudicates it, what evidence is required, or how disputes are resolved. This is a customer-facing friction point that will generate support volume on day one.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Finance", "Legal"],
      "options": [
        "A: Driver flags wrong address with photo evidence; customer support adjudicates within 24 hours.",
        "B: Automated address validation at order time eliminates most wrong-address scenarios; residual cases handled by support.",
        "C: GPS-based delivery confirmation — if driver arrived at the specified coordinates but no one was there, address is presumed correct but customer unavailable (different policy)."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B as primary defense (validate address at order placement), with Option A as fallback for edge cases that pass validation but are still wrong (e.g., correct format, wrong unit number)."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Finance says refunds are 'processed within 3 business days.' Customer Success says driver-cancellation refunds are 'automatic.' These could be compatible (auto-initiated, 3-day processing) or contradictory (instant credit vs delayed processing). The refund experience is a critical customer touchpoint and must be precisely defined.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance", "Customer Support"],
      "options": [
        "A: All express refunds are auto-initiated immediately but take up to 3 business days to appear in customer's account (payment processor latency).",
        "B: Driver-cancellation refunds are instant credits; SLA-miss refunds require manual review and take 3 business days.",
        "C: All refunds are instant account credits, with Finance reconciliation happening internally within 3 business days."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — auto-initiation with transparent processing time. Differentiate the trigger (automatic vs reviewed) from the settlement time (payment processor dependent). Communicate expected timeline to customer at refund initiation."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No operating hours or availability windows defined for express delivery. Can a customer place an express order at 2 AM? What about holidays? What happens if a warehouse is at capacity or closed? The 15-minute pickup SLA is meaningless if the warehouse is not staffed.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Warehouse Operations", "Product Management"],
      "options": [
        "A: Express available only during warehouse operating hours (need warehouse hours defined).",
        "B: Express available 24/7 with dynamic availability based on real-time warehouse and driver capacity.",
        "C: Fixed express hours (e.g., 8 AM - 10 PM) regardless of warehouse schedule."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A initially — tied to warehouse hours with clear UI indication of availability. Expand to Option B as operational maturity grows."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No fallback defined for when no express-eligible driver (4+ stars) is available. The system could accept an express order and then fail the 15-minute pickup SLA because no qualified driver exists in the area. Need a pre-order availability check or a degradation policy.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations", "Drivers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Do not show express option if no eligible driver is available within estimated pickup range.",
        "B: Accept order optimistically and escalate immediately if no driver claims within 5 minutes.",
        "C: Allow lower-rated drivers as fallback after N minutes, with operations approval."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — do not offer what you cannot fulfill. Check driver availability before showing express as an option. This prevents SLA misses at the source rather than managing them after the fact."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
